data.title: September Inflation Pleases Market, But Caution Lies Ahead
data.description:
Introduction

Earlier today, the Bureau of Labor Statistics released the September Consumer Price Index report. The report is one of the leading measures of inflation in the economy. The report noted that on a month-to-month basis, inflation rose by 0.3%. When removing food and energy from the equation, core inflation rose by 0.2%. On a year-over-year basis, inflation is higher by 3.0% and without food and energy, core inflation was also 3.0% higher. While the monthly changes came in a little lighter than expected, inflation remains well above the Federal Reserve’s long-term target, although I suspect it will not change the Central Bank’s plans.

How Does September’s Inflation Report Stack Up Historically?

While equity markets surged on the lighter than expected inflation report, there isn’t any clear evidence to suggest that a 2% target is in sight. Despite being an average monthly core inflation reading compared to the last twelve months, September’s headline inflation reading was also the fourth highest monthly level in the last twelve months.

On a year over year basis, headline inflation is at 3%, which is the highest level since January. Headline inflation fell to 2.3% back in April but has increased in four out of the last five months. Core inflation on a year-over-year basis fell to 2.8% in March but has slightly risen since then. Furthermore, by annualizing recent inflation data, it appears as if core inflation will be at or above 3% for next few months. Essentially, with interest rates falling, the economy will need to tolerate these inflations levels for at least the next six to twelve months.

Are Tariffs Contributing to Inflation?

Back in August, I estimated that tariffs would account for 1.1% of total inflation growth over a twelve month period. While September represents six months of data since Liberation Day, trade policy was exceptionally choppy between then and August. Despite this, there is evidence of tariff influence in recent inflation data.

Durable goods inflation, which was largely negative for two to three years after pandemic shortages caused pricing spikes, has slowly began to creep up. In September, durable goods inflation rose by 0.3% month-to-month and rose by more than 1% over the last three months. On a year over year basis, durable goods inflation came in at 1.8%, which is still manageable.

Nondurable goods, which have been more inconsistent in 2025, saw the bigger jump in September. On a month over month basis, nondurable goods inflation rose by 1.0%, the largest monthly jump in over two years. Yet, despite the large monthly increase, year over year nondurable goods inflation came in at a modest 2.0%.

It’s likely that tariffs are the largest drivers to the recent changes in goods prices, but they have not risen by enough to constitute a pain point in pricing. It is fair to note that many months remain before we can determine the full extent of tariffs on pricing and that changes in trade policy at any moment could have varying impacts.

Service Sector Inflation Continues to Moderate

The service sector was the shining light in the September inflation report as disinflation continued to ease across the sector. On a month-over-month basis, service sector inflation rose by 0.2%. This was the third lowest monthly reading in the last year. On a year-over-year basis, service sector inflation came in at 3.5%, which ties with May for the lowest level since November of 2021. Service sector inflation is about 80 basis points shy of its pre-pandemic level.

Housing has been doing its part to bring service sector inflation under control. Housing rose by 0.2% on a monthly basis in September and is up 3.9% on a year over year basis. More impressively is the progress being made in rental inflation, which was up 0.3% on a monthly basis and 3.4% on a year-over-year basis. Year-over-year rental inflation is at its lowest level since the pandemic and now sits slightly below pre-pandemic levels.

Federal Reserve Outlook and Conclusion

There is nothing in the September inflation to suggest that the Federal Reserve is going to deviate from its path laid out in September. The Fed has projected two additional rate cuts totally 50 basis points between now and the end of the year based on the underlying weakness in the labor market outweighing concerns over inflation.

I do believe that these rate cuts could come back to haunt investors in 2026 should the labor market firm back up and inflation remain above 3%. With equity markets in frothy territory and short-term rate cuts likely to fuel equities further, we could be setting ourselves up for a volatile equity market in 2026.

data.title: AI's Potential To Decimate The Jobs Market
data.description:
The AI narrative continues to power the market forward despite myriad worries around the faltering jobs markets, the government shutdown entering its fourth week, continuing trade tensions, and a falling U.S. dollar, among numerous concerns. AI has been responsible for approximately two-thirds of the market gains since the end of 2022 as well as a good chunk of recent economic growth. Names like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) and Microsoft (MSFT) have ridden this wave higher and now have larger market caps than the annual GDPs of either the U.K. or France.

I have numerous concerns about what I see as an obvious AI bubble. Among them is, will generative AI deliver the revenues to justify the hundreds of billions annually being devoted to building out AI capabilities and constructing huge data centers? Will China's efforts around AI turn out to be more cost-effective? Will electricity capacity increase enough in the short term to meet the substantial demand from these AI facilities?

However, one of my biggest concerns is the only way AI can generate the returns necessary to justify these massive capital budgets is if it results in the massive loss of jobs. And given the consumer is responsible for nearly 70% of U.S. GDP, that would tank the economy and the markets.

We are just starting to see AI's huge potential to disrupt the jobs market. It was recently leaked at Amazon (AMZN), that the online retail and web services giant plans to avoid hiring some 600,000 logistical workers in lieu of robots in the coming years. Ironically, that means potentially 600,000 consumers will have to find other employment opportunities to be able to afford products on Amazon's marketplace and Whole Foods markets.

Meta Platforms (META), which is spending tens of billions on building data centers, including a massive facility in Louisiana, just announced it was laying off 600 employees at its AI unit as it feels it had scaled up too aggressively in that business.

A Senate report earlier this month projected that AI and automation could eliminate nearly 100 million jobs across multiple sectors in the U.S. over the next decade, including 47 percent of truck drivers, 64 percent of accountants, and 89 percent of fast food and counter workers. Will new jobs be created at nearly the pace to replace these lost positions? Color me skeptical on this front.

The recent drop in job openings back to pre-pandemic levels has not affected the market yet. However, the jobs market is clearly starting to falter. Job growth, after revisions, shows less than 30,000 positions created on a monthly basis from June through August. The September ADP report showed 32,000 private sector jobs were lost last month. This was the worst showing since March 2023, it should be noted. The September BLS report has not been issued yet, as the government is in its fourth week of a shutdown.

Entry-level jobs are particularly vulnerable to the AI revolution. This is one reason that recent college graduates are having an increasingly hard time finding their first professional position, with opportunities down some 15% in 2025. Of course, this comes at a time when student loan repayments have recently been restarted, after a near four-year taxpayer-funded hiatus.

Not surprisingly, student loan delinquency and default rates are soaring. Reinstated loan payments are also adversely impacting credit scores among this population. Just over 40 million individuals owe a collective $1.8 trillion in student loans. This will obviously impact demand for high-ticket items like automobiles, homes, vacations, etc. in the quarters and years ahead.

In summary, investors have bought in fully to the current AI narrative, which has driven the markets much higher since the end of 2022. Equities now are severely overextended based on many historical valuation metrics. But will AI deliver the benefits and efficiencies needed to justify the massive investments being made to construct massive data centers and build out AI infrastructure? And if they do, will AI destroy jobs at a much faster pace than they can be replaced, and/or will this paradigm shift send the rest of the economy into a recession? A $64,000 question for investors to ponder as we head into the closing innings of 2025.

My personal view is that the unemployment rate will hit 5% in 2026, and 2027 could see 6% unemployment given the myriad challenges in the economy, especially in the residential and commercial real estate markets, which I have covered extensively in recent months. Rapidly evolving AI capabilities and their potential impacts on the jobs market are just one more economic wild card prudent investors will need to monitor closely going forward.

data.title: Aave Price Rebounds As Bullish Structure Strengthens On Buyback Optimism
data.description:
By Ashutosh Sureka

​Aave (AAVE-USD) trades at $229.32, up 3.6% in the past 24 hours, outperforming the broader market as traders rotate into high-liquidity DeFi assets. The market cap stands at $3.50B, with a fully diluted valuation of $3.67B and 24-hour trading volume at $274.8M. The daily range of $219.41–$234.35 reflects a rebound from oversold conditions, marking early strength after a multi-week decline. The price is holding above $225 support, with resistance near $235 and $255, zones that previously acted as sell-side barriers.

Technical picture

AAVE’s price action shows tentative recovery signs with increasing volume near the 20 EMA ($224.42) and 50 EMA ($224.91). The 100 EMA ($232.44) and 200 EMA ($247.31) remain higher, creating a layered resistance zone that defines the next test for bullish continuation. RSI at 55.95 shows improving strength without entering overbought levels, leaving room for short-term continuation if buying persists.

Aave price dynamics (Source: TradingView)

Higher lows have formed near $220, confirming base-building behavior and renewed interest at discounted levels. A decisive breakout above $240 would confirm a trend reversal, potentially targeting $255–$260 in the next leg. However, failure to hold $225 could reintroduce short-term consolidation between $215–$220.

Flows and derivatives positioning

On-chain data from Coinglass shows net outflows of $1.8M on October 24, suggesting mild accumulation by long-term holders as exchange reserves shrink. These outflows during early price recovery phases often precede upward extensions, hinting at whale re-entry. Market cap and price have moved in tandem since mid-October, signaling organic spot demand rather than leveraged speculation.

Despite improving structure, flow balance remains fragile, indicating traders are still quick to realize gains. A sustained rise in positive netflows over the next few sessions will be crucial to validate this rebound’s durability.

Macro and sentiment backdrop

The broader market remains cautious but improving. The Fear & Greed Index stands at 36 (Fear), reflecting gradual sentiment recovery from extreme fear levels. Bitcoin dominance near 59% still limits altcoin momentum, but DeFi majors like Aave are showing relative strength as capital rotation begins within the sector.

AAVE’s fundamentals underpin the improving outlook. The October 23 acquisition of Stable Finance expands its retail reach through simplified savings products, complementing its institutional strategy. Meanwhile, the upcoming V4 protocol upgrade aims to unify cross-chain liquidity and optimize capital efficiency, reinforcing Aave’s leadership in decentralized lending. Additionally, the DAO’s proposed $50M buyback program signals strong governance conviction, reducing circulating supply over time if implemented. Institutional participation through Maple Finance’s syrupUSDT integration adds further depth to its credit ecosystem.

Broader outlook

AAVE’s structure is improving but remains technically delicate. The bias stays bullish above $225, targeting $235–$255, with invalidation below $218. Sustained outflows and buyback execution could extend upside momentum, while a break above the 100 EMA would confirm a shift toward a stronger mid-term trend.

In earlier analysis, the $220 support zone was highlighted as pivotal for rebound validation, and current behavior aligns with that projection—holding this base is key to sustaining the ongoing recovery.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: MDST: High Yield, But Suffers In Rising Markets
data.description:
The Westwood Salient Enhanced Midstream Income ETF (NYSE:MDST) is an exchange-traded midstream energy fund that aims to provide investors with a higher level of current income than what other midstream funds are able to provide. To its credit, the yield of MDST is in fact higher than that of most other midstream exchange-traded funds, which we can see here:

Fund Name

Current Yield

Westwood Salient Enhanced Midstream Income ETF (MDST)

10.43%

Alerian MLP ETF (AMLP)

8.27%

Global X MLP & Energy Infrastructure ETF (MLPX)

4.82%

Tortoise North American Pipeline Fund (TPYP)

3.98%

First Trust North American Energy Infrastructure Fund ETF (EMLP)

3.10%

USCF Midstream Energy Income Fund ETF (UMI)

4.15%

Alerian Energy Infrastructure ETF (ENFR)

4.78%

As we can see from this selection of energy infrastructure funds, the Westwood Salient Enhanced Midstream ETF has a current yield that is more than double that of most other energy infrastructure ETFs. The only one in the list that manages to come close to matching it is the Alerian MLP ETF, and even that fund has a yield that is 216 basis points lower than MDST. That means that MDST will provide an additional $21,600 in annual income (about $1,800 per month) for every $1 million invested in it. This clearly represents a lot of income that would be left on the table if an investor were to choose one of the other funds shown in the table.

As income investors, one of our goals is to generate as much income as we reasonably can from the assets that we already possess, so the Westwood Salient Enhanced Midstream ETF certainly looks attractive in this respect. However, as with most things, there can be some trade-offs to purchasing this fund as a source of income compared to its peers, so we should be sure that we understand and are willing to make these trade-offs before purchasing shares of this fund.

The yields of many of the above funds may seem low to subscribers to Energy Profits in Dividends. After all, we regularly discuss energy infrastructure funds that use a closed-end fund structure to provide substantially higher yields than the 3% to 5% range shown for most of the funds that are included in the chart above. However, even most of these funds have lower yields than MDST. Here are the current yields of the five remaining energy infrastructure CEFs in the market:

Fund Name

Current Yield

ClearBridge Energy Midstream Opportunity Fund (EMO)

10.00%

Kayne Anderson Energy Infrastructure Fund (KYN)

8.18%

Neuberger Berman Energy Infrastructure and Income Fund (NML)

8.52%

NXG Cushing Midstream Energy Fund (SRV)

12.78%

Tortoise Energy Infrastructure Corp. (TYG)

10.29%

We can immediately see that three of these funds come very close to or even beat the yield of MDST. Two of these three funds have also managed to beat MDST’s performance on a total return basis since the fund’s inception date. The Westwood Salient Enhanced Midstream Income ETF has an inception date of April 8, 2024. This chart shows how each of the three CEFs with yields of 10% or higher has done against it since that date, assuming that investors reinvested all of the distributions that were paid out by each of the funds:

The performance of the Westwood Salient Enhanced Midstream Income ETF does not actually look that great in comparison to these funds. EMO and TYG have both managed to outperform it since inception, and both of those funds also offer very similar yields. We could make a reasonable case that both of these funds will also outperform MDST over an extended period of time due to the strategies that they each employ.

Basic Strategy Of The Westwood Salient Enhanced Midstream Income ETF

The strategy employed by the Westwood Salient Enhanced Midstream Income ETF is quite different from that employed by the various CEFs in the above chart, as well as the strategies employed by the various ETFs shown in the first chart of this article. Most energy infrastructure exchange-traded funds simply purchase C-corporations and master limited partnerships that are involved in the midstream energy sector (or utility sector, as is the case with funds such as TYG). These funds then collect the dividends and distributions paid out by the companies in their portfolios and pass them through to their investors along with any capital gains that they manage to realize (after using some of the proceeds to cover the respective fund’s own expenses). All of the above funds except for MDST use this basic strategy, although the degree to which they will hold master limited partnerships varies by fund.

The willingness of a given fund to hold master limited partnerships is important because that has a significant impact on the amount of income that the fund will earn through dividends and distributions. This is because the yields of master limited partnerships tend to be significantly higher than the yields of midstream C-corporations. For example, let us look at the yields of the largest holdings in the portfolio of AMLP, which only holds master limited partnerships. Here are the largest holdings in that particular fund:

Here are the yields of these companies:

Company Name

Current Yield

Western Midstream Partners (WES)

9.35%

MPLX LP (MPLX)

7.52%

Sunoco LP (SUN)

6.79%

Plains All American Pipeline (PAA)

9.04%

Enterprise Products Partners (EPD)

7.04%

Energy Transfer (ET)

7.83%

Hess Midstream (HESM)

8.74%

Cheniere Energy Partners (CQP)

6.00%

USA Compression Partners (USAC)

9.26%

Genesis Energy (GEL)

4.16%

We can see that with the exception of Genesis Energy, all of the ten largest positions in AMLP’s portfolio have yields of 6% or higher. Now, let us compare this with the yields of the largest companies in MLPX’s portfolio. Unlike AMLP, MLPX attempts to limit its exposure to master limited partnerships to less than 25% so it can avoid having to structure itself as a C-corporation. Here are the largest positions in this fund:

Here are the yields of these companies:

Company Name

Current Yield

The Williams Companies (WMB)

3.39%

Enbridge (ENB)

5.70%

TC Energy (TRP)

4.76%

Kinder Morgan (KMI)

4.46%

Cheniere Energy (LNG)

0.90%

ONEOK (OKE)

5.91%

Pembina Pipeline (PBA)

5.31%

MPLX (MPLX)

7.52%

DT Midstream (DTM)

3.09%

Enterprise Products Partners (EPD)

7.04%

The only two companies on this list that are master limited partnerships are MPLX and Enterprise Products Partners. All of the other companies shown on the list are structured as regular C-corporations. We can immediately see that the MLPs have substantially higher yields than the C-corporations that have similar business models. The takeaway here is that a fund’s income is correlated to the ratio between master limited partnerships and C-corporations in its portfolio. A fund with a high ratio of MLPs relative to C-corporations will earn a higher yield from its portfolio. This is the reason why AMLP has a higher yield than most of the other energy infrastructure ETFs.

Here are the largest holdings in the portfolio of the Westwood Salient Enhanced Midstream Income ETF:

There are three MLPs on the list—Energy Transfer, Enterprise Products Partners, and MPLX—which makes this fund somewhat more similar to MLPX than to AMLP. It appears that MDST is attempting to limit its exposure to master limited partnerships, which is confirmed by the fund’s fact sheet. The fact sheet includes a chart that shows the following asset allocation:

As shown, the fund only had 23.16% of its assets invested in master limited partnerships. This is not atypical among those midstream funds that are structured as registered investment companies, as tax laws forbid such funds from having more than 25% of their assets invested in master limited partnerships. From the fund’s prospectus:

The Fund may directly invest up to but not more than 25% (or such higher amount as permitted by any applicable tax diversification rules) of total assets, at the time of investment, in equity securities of MLPs.

This statement directly confirms that the fund is trying to limit its MLP exposure due to the 25% rule. As such, we can expect that the fund’s portfolio will have a lower yield than AMLP’s portfolio. This basically means that this fund must be obtaining the money necessary to cover its distribution from some other source.

The fact that the fund has the word “enhanced” in its name tells us what this other source of income is. An enhanced equity fund is typically a fund that sells covered call options against some proportion of the assets in its portfolio. The premiums from these option sales provide a synthetic dividend that allows the fund to generate a higher yield from the assets in its portfolio than the equities themselves provide. This is exactly what MDST is doing, as stated in the prospectus:

The Fund will engage in covered call writing. The Fund currently expects to write call options for the purpose of generating realized gains and distributable cash flow to investors. The Fund will only write call options on securities that the Fund holds in its portfolio (i.e., covered calls). Each long security will likely have a corresponding call option written on it.

MDST sells covered calls, which is generally considered to be the safest of all of the option strategies because, in the worst-case scenario, the fund simply ends up being forced to sell assets that it already owns. However, this strategy also has the effect of capping the upside potential of the equity securities. The fund (and by extension investors in the fund) will not be able to benefit from any gains in the price of the midstream equities above the strike price. This could potentially cause this fund to underperform some of the other midstream funds during a bull market.

We already saw that MDST has been struggling to keep up with some of the midstream CEFs since its inception. After all, the biggest selling point of the covered call strategy is that it results in a higher level of income, and through the use of leverage, the midstream closed-end funds are able to keep up with MDST in terms of yield. Thus, the fund’s capped upside could be preventing it from matching the performance of these other funds. The fund’s capped upside potential also appears to be preventing it from keeping up with some of the energy infrastructure ETFs on a total return basis. Here is how the fund has compared to its ETF peers since inception:

On a total return basis, all of them managed to beat MDST except for AMLP. This occurred despite the fact that MDST has a higher yield than any of the midstream exchange-traded funds. The reason for this is very simple—all of the peers managed to achieve greater share price appreciation since their capital gains are not artificially limited due to the covered call strategy. This is likely to be the case during most bull market periods. However, the Westwood Salient Enhanced Midstream ETF might do better than the other funds if midstream corporations and midstream partnerships are either flat or declining during a given period. This is because the option premiums that MDST receives will offset some of the equity price declines. This has not been the case since the fund’s inception, though, and clearly it has not helped the fund. Thus, at least since the fund’s inception, investors in the Westwood Salient Enhanced Midstream Income ETF are essentially sacrificing total return in exchange for a higher level of income.

The website for MDST does suggest that the fund is not giving up all of the upside potential from the equities in its portfolio, despite the fact that the prospectus states that all of the securities that it owns will have options written against them. We can see this in the website’s claim that the fund is only writing options that are 4% to 5% out of the money that expire within one month. It is pretty rare for most midstream stocks to rise 4% or more in a month, so this suggests that a lot of the options that this fund writes are going to expire worthless. However, this is still sacrificing a greater amount of upside potential than the peer funds do, and we should not ignore that.

Near-Term Prospects

In a recent article posted to Energy Profits in Dividends, I posited that upside potential could be limited in the near-term for most midstream funds. The biggest reason for this is energy prices. Historically, the equity prices of midstream companies have exhibited a fairly strong correlation with West Texas Intermediate crude oil despite the fact that the revenues and cash flows of midstream companies are not affected much by changes in crude oil prices. We can see this correlation somewhat by comparing the spot price of West Texas Intermediate crude oil to the Alerian MLP Index (MLPB) since the start of this year:

In this chart, the spot price of West Texas Intermediate crude oil is represented by the black line, whereas the blue line denotes the performance of the Alerian MLP Index. While the two do not exhibit a perfect correlation, we can see that in most cases, the index rose whenever crude oil spot prices rose and vice versa. The Alerian MLP Index is one of the few equity indices that is actually down this year, and the decline in energy prices is one of the reasons for this.

It is not likely that we will see a significant upswing in energy prices in the near future. The Organization of Petroleum Exporting Countries appears determined to keep energy prices somewhat suppressed, as it has been loosening the production curbs that it first implemented back in late 2023. This has been a contributing factor to the current oversupply in the energy space, and this supply glut has been pressuring prices downward. After all, the price of crude oil is largely a function of supply and demand, and whenever supply increases more rapidly than demand (the situation that has been occurring over the past year), the price of crude oil can be expected to decline. It appears that the member nations of OPEC are comfortable with keeping the supply glut in place as well, as Kuwait, one of the members of OPEC, stated yesterday that the international organization will increase production if the United States imposes additional sanctions on major Russian oil producers.

It is unclear why OPEC seems to want lower energy prices, particularly since some of its members are dependent on oil prices being well above $60 per barrel in order to balance their national budgets. Saudi Arabia is a prime example of this, as analysts believe that the country needs oil prices to be at $80.90 per barrel on average in order to balance its government budget as the country prepares to spend a substantial amount of money on infrastructure in an attempt to wean itself off of a crude oil-focused economy.

Despite this need, Saudi Arabia has been one of the biggest proponents of maintaining the current oversupply in the energy markets. Most reports say that either the member nations of OPEC are deliberately keeping the price of oil down in order to prevent American shale from becoming a larger participant in the global oil market, or that the organization is trying to punish nations such as Kazakhstan that consistently exceed their production quotas.

The exact reasoning for the current strategy may be known only to them, but it seems likely that the organization’s current strategy will keep energy prices suppressed for quite some time. Due to the correlation between oil prices and midstream equities, it is likely that the upside potential of most midstream equities is limited for the time being.

As was mentioned earlier, the Westwood Salient Enhanced Midstream Income ETF employs a covered call strategy, and such strategies frequently outperform during periods of flat or declining equity prices. The fact that energy prices are likely to remain weak in the near term could, therefore, suggest that this fund could be a decent holding until energy prices begin to rise.

After all, given the current 10.43% yield, the MDST fund should be able to provide investors with a reasonable return even if its share price remains stable for a while. We do not need much in the way of capital gains to be earning a respectable return from this fund, as the distribution itself provides that.

Potential Liquidity Concerns

The Westwood Salient Enhanced Midstream ETF is a relatively new fund, and it does not appear to be a particularly well-known one. As of the time of writing, the fund only has 643 followers on Seeking Alpha, compared to 35,570 for the much larger AMLP. More importantly, though, MDST does not appear to have a very high level of liquidity. The fund only has an average daily volume of 45,967 shares, which is about $1.18 million at the current share price. This could be problematic for anyone who wishes to place an order that is larger than a few thousand dollars or so, since a large order could move the share price of the ETF before the authorized participant has time to react and keep the share price in line with the value of the fund’s underlying assets. For most investors, it will probably make more sense to space out their buy orders to avoid causing the price to move against them.

The fund’s daily volume represents about 0.76% of the fund’s $157.47 million assets under management, so it appears that many investors are choosing to buy and hold the shares for income rather than trading them. This is a very reasonable way to use this fund, as, unlike leveraged ETFs, it is not intended as a trading vehicle and should be fine to hold overnight.

MDST Expense Ratio

The fact sheet for MDST states that the fund has an expense ratio of 0.80%, which is not too bad for an actively managed ETF.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the Westwood Salient Enhanced Midstream Income ETF is a little-followed exchange-traded fund that provides its investors with a way to gain midstream energy exposure along with a substantially higher yield than a typical midstream ETF provides. The fund achieves its income goals by writing out-of-the-money covered calls against the midstream corporations and master limited partnerships in its portfolio. This strategy can be somewhat of a mixed blessing, as it does provide investors with an increased yield but at the expense of sacrificing upside potential.

As such, we can expect this fund to underperform its peers during bull markets, but it could outperform during flat or declining markets. Therefore, this could be a decent fund for the current market environment, as oil prices seem likely to remain suppressed for a while, and it is hard to see any near-term upside catalyst for midstream energy companies.

data.title: AAR Corp.: In A Perfect Setup For Margin Expansion And A Premium Valuation
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Aerospace has been one of the most interesting industries to follow year-to-date. I've previously covered companies that operate on the leasing segment of this industry and companies that operate on the aftermarket segment. Both segments continue to benefit from the tailwind of aircraft shortages. AAR Corp (NYSE:AIR) is one such company. Operating in the aftermarket segment of the aerospace industry, AIR supplies airlines with OEM and USM (used serviceable materials) parts, but also AIR is one of the major maintenance, repair, and overhaul (MRO) service providers in the space. Using this vertically integrated structure, AIR is able to offer affordable parts and MRO services to both its commercial and government customers.

For the MRO services, AIR mainly focuses on airframe maintenance, which is why in most of its facilities you'll find hangars that are used to tear down and inspect purchased aircraft so that individual parts can be serviced, certified, and then either used as USM or as part of the MRO channel. On the other hand, its closest peer, VSE Corporation (VSEC) instead of purchasing a whole aircraft, normally purchases individual parts that it then uses for its MRO services. VSEC doesn't maintain or service whole airframes and engines; instead, it focuses on engine accessories and aircraft components.

Even though both companies operate at a smaller scale compared to their peer HEICO (HEI), they do serve an important role in the aftermarket business, and AIR's management has expressed intentions to grow the company to the point of producing its own PMA parts, directly competing with HEI.

In the last 3 years, all three companies have outperformed the S&P 500, with AIR lagging behind HEI by 10% and VSEC massively outperforming with close to a 300% performance. I estimate more upside is to be expected from AIR as management is actively working on expanding the firm's margin and aggressively enabling AIR to take market share in the parts distribution business. Judging by the current AIR valuation that is in line with its sector median, I think this presents a good entry point for investors who want to bet on margin expansion.

Due to the slow aircraft production, airlines are forced to use older planes to support the increasing demand for commercial travel. This has resulted in the average global fleet age increasing by almost a full year in 2024. This is a tailwind for the MRO business, as most of this aging fleet will soon come due for maintenance and overhaul services. In the full year of 2025, the MRO market is expected to grow to $199 billion, surpassing the 2019 peak by 12%.

Unfortunately for AIR, this surging demand hasn't been significantly beneficial, as all its hangars are at near capacity. On the latest earnings call, management did update investors on the progress of 2 additional MRO facilities in Miami and Oklahoma that are estimated to add 15% in capacity. Once these additions are completed, I believe we'll start to see massive growth in the MRO segment.

Financial Analysis

In the past 20 years, AIR was able to grow the parts supply segment from $629 million to $1.7 billion, returning a CAGR of 4.88%, and was able to grow the MRO segment from $90 million to $1.08 billion, returning a CAGR of 12.5%.

The parts supply business remains the biggest revenue contributor and has managed to expand gross margins at a steady rate from 15% to 19%. On the flip side, the MRO business has witnessed contractions in its margin profile, shrinking from 25% to being in line with the parts supply business at 19%. To me this is counterintuitive; most service businesses tend to have higher margins, as they are associated with high switching costs, giving the servicer more pricing power, which offsets the inherent limited scale.

In the effort of expanding margin, the firm divested the landing gear facility, which has been a margin laggard for years. This divestiture generated the firm $48 million in cash and was margin accretive. I expect management to continue the same effort of divesting low-margin segments in order to improve the earnings profile of the firm.

The parts supply business not only accounts for the majority of AIR's revenue but also shows high topline and bottom line performance, with the parts distribution segment maintaining above 20% YoY growth rates. The reason this segment of the business is high value accretive has to do with the contractual agreement AIR signs with OEMs. When OEMs choose AIR to be their exclusive independent distributor, they are not allowed to distribute the same part through other distributors, and AIR is not allowed to sell competing parts. OEMs are highly dependent on AIR's distribution, as this is the channel that allows their parts to be sold at a higher margin.

The USM segment shows disappointing growth on a relative basis, and this is due to the high upfront cost associated with it. For AIR to produce USM parts, it must first purchase used aircraft or engines, then tear them down, inspect and service the individual parts that later get to be categorized as USM. Given the low production rate of new aircraft, AIR is forced to purchase used aircraft at a premium since these deals are highly competitive right now.

2025 Fiscal Year Income Statement

AIR has entered its 2026 fiscal year; before looking at the first quarter, I think it would be interesting to look at the previous fiscal year's performance. For the full year of 2025, revenue grew 20% to $2.78 billion, bolstered by 14% growth in the parts supply business, a 38% increase in the MRO business, an 8% increase in integrated solutions, and a 44% increase from expeditionary income. Cost of revenue increased by 20%, resulting in gross profits growing 19% to $527.7 million and landing gross margins at 19%. Operating expenses increased by 9%, while interest and other expenses increased by 106% (caused by the new debt raised to finance the acquisition of Triumph product support), resulting in operating income increasing 43% to $185.2 million and pretax declining 33% to $38.9 million. Income tax increased by 120%, leading to net income decreasing 73% to $12.5 million and landing profit margins at 0.5%.

Q1 2026 Income Statement

The first quarter results of 2026 came with a significant improvement in the margin profile. Revenue increased 12% to $739.6 million, beating estimates by $50.15 million. The growth was slightly offset by the MRO segment decreasing by 1%; however, when we exclude the impact of the landing gear divestiture, MRO actually grew 8% YoY to $214.6 million. Operating and other expenses decreased by 7% and 3%, respectively, resulting in operating income increasing 50% to $64.9 million and pretax income increasing 89% to $47 million. Net income increased 91% to $34.4 million. This impressive performance landed operating margins at 8.8% and net profit margins at 4.6%.

Q1 2026 Cash Flow statement

Despite the earnings showing significant improvement, on a cash flow basis, AIR appears to be spending a huge amount of cash on inventory, which resulted in the negative operating cash flow of $44.9 million. Management perceives stocking more inventory as an investment to catapult the future growth prospect of the business. From my perspective, this is not necessarily bearish; if the parts shortage continues to persist or even get worse given the supply chain disruptions caused by the tariff events, AIR could be pulling forward the demand of these parts, which is a smart hedge against increasing input costs for MRO but could also give it a price advantage in the event of inventory destocking for the Parts Supply business.

Q1 2026 Balance Sheet

From a pure cash perspective, AIR's balance sheet is slightly concerning. $80 million vs. $1.02 billion in debt is just not a good look in my opinion. The good news, though, is that the majority of the debt was recently raised, so the earliest debt maturity is due in Dec of 2027. This gives the firm enough time to deleverage without near-term insolvency risks. Current assets increased by 10%, largely bolstered by a 15% increase in inventories and a 17% increase in receivables. Total assets increased by 5%, while total liabilities increased by 7%, leading to shareholder's equity increasing 3% to $1.25 billion.

Quantitative Analysis

AIR vs. Peers' Growth grades

AIR received an A+ grade for its growth performance relative to its sector, reflecting high performance on both the top and the bottom line. AIR's forward revenue growth rate is 12.71% vs. its sector median of 4.89%, and its forward EBITDA growth rate is 20.10% vs. the sector median of 6.66%. Overall, we can see that most companies are performing well in the growth factor, which is mostly a result of high demand coming from commercial customers.

AIR vs. Peers' Profitability grades

When looking at the profitability grades, this is where we see some lag relative to the sector median. AIR's gross and net margins are at 19.04% and 1%, while the sector median lands at 31.19% and 6.06%, respectively. This is a wide difference that can only narrow if management does an outstanding job in making value accretive investments, both organically and inorganically, while divesting low-performing segments.

We can see that both on profit margin and on ROE, HEI surpasses both companies by a wide margin, with AIR continuing to lag behind.

AIR vs. Peers' Valuation grades

I would like to believe that it is the profitability profile that makes AIR to trade at a massive discount to its peers. Prior to 2024, AIR's PE multiple was slightly above VSEC's multiple, but since then VSEC's multiple has continued to expand while AIR's has remained stagnant.

On a sectoral basis, AIR trades at fair value, with a C grading given by the Seeking Alpha quant rating. However, when we look at the distribution, we can see that most companies are concentrated on a premium multiple that gives them an F rating. AIR maintains a forward EV/EBITDA ratio of 11.74, while the sector median is at 12.41. I believe this multiple is a nice entry point for a stock that is slowly expanding margins while maintaining high topline performance.

Risks

One major risk associated with this name is a massive stock dilution that can be caused by the company deciding to enter stock-based acquisition deals. Recent acquisitions have mostly been all-cash deals that forced the company to issue more debt. The company has recently made an announcement to issue more shares as a way to fund more acquisitions and to deleverage its balance sheet; if not managed well, this could dilute existing investors, which might make the stock less attractive. The chart below is showing a recent uptick in outstanding shares, which have been muted since 2015. I caution investors to pay attention to how the firm manages the issuance of new stock.

Conclusion

In conclusion, I think AIR is an interesting stock that is poised to outperform once the company has managed to expand its margin and maintain a solid growth rate. Even though the firm divested the landing gear business, the MRO segment continues to be a margin dragger; however, I expect this to change once the 2 MRO facilities are completed, which will increase AIR's capacity. At these prices, I don't see any risks of a massive drawdown, but further upside if margins continue to expand. I rate AIR a buy.

data.title: September Consumer Price Index Less Than Expected, But Still Warm
data.description:
By David W. Berson, Ph.D.

The September CPI rose by 0.3 percent in September, a bit less than the market's expectations of 0.4 percent. The core CPI (removing the volatile food and energy components) also climbed slightly less than expected, up by 0.2 percent. These monthly increases pushed the 12-month trend rates up to 3.0 percent for both measures.

Not as hot as feared, but the trend rates both have a "3 handle" and that can't be viewed as favorable. It is a full percentage point above the Federal Reserve's long-term goal of 2.0 percent and moving in the wrong direction. This is the only official inflation data that will be released until the federal government shutdown is over -- and it's not clear when that will be.

The PCE price indices, which are broader than the CPI and preferred by the Fed as inflation measures relative to the CPI, will not be released until the shutdown ends. But several of the regional Federal Reserve banks will release their computations of inflation central tendency measures based on the details in today's CPI report later today. Most economists view these inflation measures as superior to either the CPI or PCE inflation measures -- and they have been running a bit hotter.

The FOMC meets this week with much less data on the economy and inflation than it would normally have. Despite the CPI trend rates running at 3.0 percent, the CME's FedWatch tool shows that markets have a 99 percent probability of a 25-basis point cut in the federal funds rate at that time. There is a similar expectation for another Fed rate cut at the December FOMC meeting -- with even more rate cuts in 2026.

Markets expect the Fed to ease despite hotter-than-goal inflation, presumably because the job market has cooled and maximum employment is the other part of the Fed's mandate. If the economy is cooling dangerously, then early Fed easing is appropriate. But if supply-side shocks (mostly from tariffs) are pushing inflation higher, then significant Fed easing runs the risk of monetizing those shocks and keeping inflation elevated (as we saw in the 1970s).

This is an especially fraught time not to have a steady stream of economic data to help the Fed make correct policy decisions. But we are likely to stay in this data fog for a while.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Novo Nordisk: Decade-Low Valuation Meets A Billion-Patient Market
data.description:
I am initiating Novo Nordisk coverage with a Strong Buy as I have been accumulating the stock on multiple occasions ever since it fell below the DKK 400 mark. Just this week, I made another purchase. The rationale as to why the stock appeared on my radar was:

  1. Strategic diversification outside of the US,
  2. Tactical diversification away from heavily AI-related tech holdings, as obesity and diabetes treatment is uncorrelated to shifts in digital innovation, or demand might even increase amid robots taking over physical work, and
  3. The 70%+ drop from last year’s all-time high.

Upon further research, my case for Novo Nordisk builds on two main pillars:

  1. I view ongoing competition with Eli Lilly and political pricing scrutiny in the US as mid-term noise. The global number of people in need of treatment, still low penetration, and the rather early stage of medication development on a longer time scale suggest that there is room for multiple players to grow and temporary “winners” of the competition will rotate regularly.
  2. Valuation is now so far below pre-Ozempic or -Wegovy hype levels, that essentially major shrinkage of these businesses is priced in, not at all accounting for pipeline potential.

Now, do I believe that we, as a society, would be better off just moving more and eating less (junk)? Sure. But I also believe that now that we have lots of increasingly chronic and potentially fatal man-made diseases, why not use the research we have to improve and save lives that took a wrong turn health-wise – and to facilitate this. That is most likely the consensus viewpoint in modern societies. Thus, I continue to bet on Novo Nordisk.

What A Year It Has Been For Novo Nordisk

Sales and EBIT outlook for the fiscal year 2025 have been slashed multiple times during the year on a constant exchange rate [CER] basis. At the beginning of the year, management has targeted an EBIT growth mid-point of 23% for 2025, which was revised to 20% after Q1, down to 13% after Q2 and finally down to 7% during Q3 amid the announcement of major restructuring. The latest revision, however, includes one-off charges. Management also communicated the intention to cut 11% of the company’s global workforce, leading to annualized savings of DKK 8 billion by the end of 2026. This measure could support margins in the realm of 2-3 percentage points.

Besides the stock price and the FY outlook, leadership has been shaken up as well this year, adding another layer of uncertainty. Ex-CEO Lars Fruergard Jørgensen stepped down and was followed by Maziar Mike Doustdar at the helm. Now, in mid-November, an extraordinary general meeting will take place to elect a new chair and new board members, as chairman Helge Lund and other members are leaving the board after a disagreement with the Novo Nordisk Foundation – the company’s largest shareholder (28% of share capital, 77% of votes). Former CEO Lars Rebien Sørensen will be nominated as interim chairman for two to three years with the aim of supporting current management as well as to find a longer-term chair.

Pharma Pricing – Both Noise And Risk

The risk of politicization of drug pricing is not at all new and is inherent to the business model. Investors in the health care and pharma sectors should always be aware of that. With that, renewed pressure out of the White House requesting severe price cuts for weight-loss drugs – while potentially serious – is part of the cost of doing business in this realm. Beyond that, threatening gestures have become very common nowadays, and I am not going to speculate about their seriousness at this point. As such, I have no choice other than to acknowledge it as a risk factor. Furthermore, while such scrutiny might apply to both companies, the European Novo Nordisk would certainly have a political disadvantage on the US market against the American Eli Lilly (LLY). Just recently, Novo Nordisk hired a new head of corporate affairs with decades of experience at Sanofi, Pfizer and AbbVie. This might underscore the politicization of the pricing discussion, leaving me no choice but to leave it at that. But now – back to business.

Pillar Number 1: A Global Market Big Enough For All

Novo Nordisk’s and Eli Lilly’s race for market shares is happening within a market that is not even remotely saturated. Out of 934 million people with obesity (BMI >30), only 4 million (~0.5%) are currently being treated with branded anti-obesity medication such as those from Novo Nordisk or Eli Lilly. And out of 589 million people with diabetes, only around 15% are living with “good control” of blood glucose according to Novo Nordisk. Until 2050, Novo Nordisk expects 853 million adults to suffer from diabetes amid a rising prevalence (1 in 8, up from 1 in 9 in 2024), especially in Emerging Markets and Asia. This sheer scale of a physical, long-term human health crisis is structural and might even accelerate as AI-driven robotic gradually takes on physical human work.

What sets Novo Nordisk’s currently difficult standing in the US market especially into perspective is that 90% of people with obesity or suffering from diabetes are outside the US. Beyond that, several other widespread indications, such as liver diseases with hundreds of millions of people, as well as rare diseases, offer wildcards for Novo Nordisk’s broad research pipeline.

Sure, Eli Lilly’s Tirzepatide-based drugs have caught up significantly to Novo Nordisk’s semaglutide-based ones. As a result, Novo Nordisk’s Ozempic and Wegovy are losing market share to the American competitor, which is partly offset by both the Diabetes and the Obesity care markets growing as a whole.

Furthermore, the company’s US dependence is also notable with a 56% company-wide revenue share during H1 2025, which is especially high for Ozempic (69%) and Wegovy (67%) – Novo Nordisk’s blockbuster drugs accounting for 42% and 24% of total revenues, respectively. At the same time, however, I would like to shift the focus on above-average international (non-US) growth of 19% in H1, driven by Wegovy growing 335% outside of the US during the period. The chart below summarizes this trade-off between still-high US-dependence [con] and strong international growth [pro] at the same time.

Pillar Number 2: Multi-Decade Valuation Lows

Novo Nordisk’s TTM P/E of around 13 to 14x is the lowest it has ever been over the 20-year timeline depicted below. Over this period, the company’s average P/E was 23x. Furthermore, I have examined the average valuation from 2005 to 2020 – roughly the time before the hype around major blockbuster approvals like Ozempic or Wegovy began shaping the stock price. During that time, it was valued at an average of 21x earnings, followed by an average of 30x during the frenzy from 2021 until today. Thus, no matter how we put it, based on P/E, the company has never been so cheap in the last 20 years.

Compare that to competitor Eli Lilly below. Similarly, this stock traded at a valuation average of 21x earnings over 20 years (Novo Nordisk 23x). On a TTM basis, Eli Lilly’s P/E is 52x (Novo Nordisk 14x), against the backdrop of it arguably having the upper hand for now in the market and thus growing faster. Nevertheless, even on a 2028 consensus forward basis, Eli Lilly remains significantly more expensive than Novo Nordisk with a 2028 FWD P/E of 20x (Novo Nordisk: 11x).

Novo Nordisk’s free cash flow, on the other hand, is currently significantly under pressure – not due to low operating cash flow, which is at all-time highs, but due to elevated capex amid production buildout in order to avoid delivery shortages that had benefitted the market for compounded drugs in the past. TTM EV to FCF is 25x and expected to jump to 43x amid these investments, but according to consensus FCF, drops to 16x by 2028, implying a 6% FCF yield. Eli Lilly faces the same issue of high necessary capex.

Strong Buy

Concluding, Novo Nordisk’s research meets “millions of untreated patients”, putting mid-term competition with Eli Lilly or compounded drugs into perspective. Obesity and diabetes remain high growth markets, whose treatment requires physical manufacturing and real-life biology - which can be supported, but not replaced by AI. As of today, for the short- to mid-term, markets expect a local advantage for the US-competitor, while from my perspective, this market might still be in its early stages – too early to already declare winners. Important upcoming developments would be admission of Oral Sema for obesity care which would simplify administration (already filed), or CagriSema Phase 3 successes. Beyond that, Novo Nordisk diversifies into other areas like Alzheimer’s or liver diseases, including the acquisition of Akero Therapeutics.

With Q3 earnings ahead on November 5, I expect the bar has already been set low after multiple downward revisions for the FY. Given 18% EBIT growth during H1 and the latest targeted mid-point of 7% for FY 2025, the H2 EBIT is implicitly expected to decline or stagnate. In the bigger picture, valuation far below any historical average already adequately accounts for political and competitive risks. As such, and against the backdrop of my own diversification efforts amid AI-heavyweights, this European pharma giant is a Strong Buy for me.

“Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best AI-Resistant Idea investment competition, which runs through November 10. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!”

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Paradox Of RING: It Multiplies Gains And Risks
data.description:
On iShares MSCI Global Gold Miners ETF (NASDAQ:RING), I see a still positive technical setup, unfortunately, however, an unbalanced fundamental picture. And it’s a bit the view I also shared in this latest coverage, where honestly the HOLD rating matched little with the subsequent explosion (+51.9%).

But I still think so, the business of gold mining companies is cyclical, and the price action follows with greater explosiveness (in positive and negative) the trend of the price of gold. The latter, instead, does not follow fundamental logics, often not even macro, but predominantly market (demand and supply). Regarding gold, demand has been supported by debasement, a trend that apparently has also been quickly questioned by the market, judging by this week’s selling.

Will the long-awaited negative cycle on RING therefore begin? In my opinion, indeed, there could be a short-term negative catalyst. At the same time, it would be synonymous with a serious lack of memory not to remember all the support of central banks to the price action of XAU. So how does it make sense to move?

Good question. Let’s go in order.

What is RING?

With an AUM of $1.45 billion and an expense ratio of 0.39%, RING offers a solution to participate in the gold mining market in a very vertical way (99.82% in gold miners).

RING in fact, replicates the performance of the MSCI ACWI Select Gold Miners Investable Market Index, that is, the main global stocks related to the gold mining market. The index is built through a sampling mechanism with a predefined capping method: no issuer above 25% exposure, the sum of issuers with a weight greater than 5% cannot exceed 50% cumulatively, and exclusion of companies with gold price hedging above 10% of the business.

Result

All this makes it very anchored to the price of gold (GLD), even though RING has become increasingly close to the S&P 500 thanks to its hybrid equity nature (not commodities).

RING therefore has a historical beta of about 0.50. This makes it a meeting point between these two markets: the ETF statistically amplifies gold’s movements, both positive and negative.

For this reason, it maintains very high volatility, with a 3-year std. dev of 31.46. However, this element is also reflected in strong momentum, with a 3-year total return of 247%.

To be clear, the price of gold instead grew by 144% over the same period with a 3-year std. dev of 15.

Confirming somewhat the multiplicative nature of RING’s risk and performance.

Why is it so volatile?

It must be remembered that RING’s holdings have a cyclical nature. The ETF is extremely exposed to gold cycles due to the capital-intensive structure of its holdings.

The capital-intensive structure of the ETF’s holdings makes the ETF itself highly exposed to gold cycles.

In other words, RING is exposed to companies with gold exposure below 10%, but it must be remembered that the costs to sustain mining activity increase proportionally to the price of gold. This means that a decrease in the price of gold will strongly affect the companies inside RING.

Holding distribution

It currently includes 55 holdings, all belonging to the gold mining segment; however, the top 10 holdings represent about 66% of the total distribution, so we can say that it is a very concentrated ETF.

At a geographical level, there is a strong exposure to Canada and Australia, which is significant, since we are talking about company stocks, not passive exposures to the yellow metal as in physically or synthetically replicated ETFs or ETPs. Here, the political dynamics of these countries influence fundamental expectations and therefore the price action.

In particular, I would focus on the top 3 holdings. Together they weigh more than 30%, and looking at the forward valuations, AEM is the most unbalanced both on TTM and forward metrics. Overall, they are in line with the sector mean. What mostly concerns me, in fact, is the sector mean itself, on which the market may have pressed too hard on the accelerator. Let me explain better.

My opinion

When sentiment rises, the market amplifies structural growth, such as that driven by central bank debasement, through speculation on ETFs like RING (return multipliers) or on the GLD options market. This is shown by the fact that RING has a P/E of 32x and that both HV and IV on XAU options are high.

And I know well that mining companies should be evaluated using different valuation metrics, but it’s still a clue that the companies inside this ETF are almost priced as if they were growth stocks, with a compound EPS growth effect, when in reality they have a cyclical business.

Focusing on a ratio that, in my opinion, is much more consistent with the capital-intensive nature of RING’s holdings, we can see that even the EV/EBITDA trades around 10x, still a value above the average.

And as long as the price of gold continues to rise at this pace, it’s actually an acceptable premium (as analyzed here). But…

Risk

What happens if the price of gold starts to contract? Simply, FCF margins collapse rapidly. Therefore, the risk shifts to gold’s growth expectations. What’s the issue? One could open an endless chapter on the reasons supporting gold’s growth, from debasement to political risks, up to the present day. What I believe could interrupt the growth pace of recent years (above average), in my opinion, should rather be sought in the technical nature of the market during speculative phases.

I focus on the spike in the GVZ, the gold volatility index. The 6% drop generated a rather unusual rise in this indicator, which in my opinion is a sign of strong speculation in the options market (something that also worried me in the last rating).

Considering that both HV and IV are high, in my view there’s double downward pressure, both new interest in selling (cheap options) and potential new hedges. And since gold follows market dynamics, an increase in supply would really be the only thing that could sink expected CAGRs, leading to a revaluation of RING’s holdings.

Conclusion

That said, I like RING: it’s a multiplier (in positive and negative) of the price of gold, and I recognize that in certain portfolios and for certain needs it can be an attractive solution even compared to GLD. However, considering the imbalance in fundamental terms for RING and the technical instability on gold, I think the right rating is HOLD. Even at the cost of sustaining a positive opportunity cost.

data.title: Hedging The Dollar: Bitcoin, Gold, Or What?
data.description:
The U.S. dollar has been falling. Gold and Bitcoin have been rising. Which will protect the U.S. dollar in your portfolio? Or should it be something else? What about the U.S. dollar index, or just the Eurodollar currency, or even Coinbase (the crypto exchange) or the S&P?

I’m going to use BTC-USD data from 2017, when bitcoin futures (BTC) started trading, and because more recent volatility is important. But all the data will be cash prices, not back-adjusted futures, except for Coinbase (COIN) and SPY (the S&P ETF). Gold is physical gold prices, and DX is the futures symbol for the dollar index.

The motivation for this is whether gold or bitcoin is a better investment. Perhaps you don’t need my analysis to make your decision, but there are a few points that you should consider.

  1. Gold collapsed after 1980, and it took 25 years before it moved up. It could decline again and stay down. Gold is not normally volatile, but the recent run-up makes it more vulnerable.
  2. BTC-USD has no intrinsic value, nor does any of the cryptocurrencies. Its price is entirely based on demand, or lack of demand. It can be volatile.
  3. Which of the two will protect against losses in the U.S. dollar? Or, does it matter?
  4. Which offers the best diversification to an S&P portfolio?
  5. Is it better to trade the exchange, COIN, than BTC-USD?

History of Gold Prices

I was trading in 1980 when gold peaked along with silver. It coincided with 18% interest rates, something I hope won’t happen again. It then languished for 30 years until just after the 2008 financial crisis. In Chart 1, it certainly looks different now.

Chart 1. Physical gold prices from 1978 through October 21, 2025. (Source: Commodity Systems Inc. data)

Note that having reached nearly $2,000, it retracted to $1,000, a 50% decline. A reminder that nothing is without risk.

History of Bitcoin Prices

Realistically, Bitcoin only goes back to 2017, when futures began trading, giving us about 8 years of data. BTC-USD has always been volatile and is not likely to change. Chart 2 shows both physical gold (on the right scale) and BTC-USD (on the left scale), both adjusted to 100 on 1/2/2017. A first glance shows the remarkable return of BTC-USD and the low volatility of gold.

Chart 2. BTC-USD and physical gold cash prices. (Source: Commodity Systems Inc. data)

Hedging the U.S. Dollar

Gold has always been seen as a hedge against a declining U.S. dollar. BTC-USD originally advertised itself as a “currency,” but it’s not clear just what it is.

The dollar gains and loses based on the U.S. economy. When investors buy U.S. treasuries or buy U.S. stocks, they convert their currency to U.S. dollars. When we sell wheat or soybeans for export, we increase the value of the dollar.

The opposite can happen. If investors are not happy with U.S. policies, they abandon the dollar, they don’t buy U.S. grains, they pull their investments out of the stock markets. The dollar declines. That has happened recently.

This is a good time to see whether gold, BTC-USD, or some other market can offset the loss in the dollar. First, let’s look at the U.S. Dollar Index (DX futures, underlying cash value), a composite of major currencies. When under 100, the dollar is weak. When above 100, it is strong. In general, the dollar has held in a neutral range but is clearly weaker than it was in late 2022.

Chart 3. The dollar index, underlying futures cash prices (DX-ICE).

But perhaps looking only at the euro currency may be easier to see. The way the price is read is “how many USD per euro.” When the price rises, the dollar weakens. In Chart 4, the weakest U.S. dollar value was 125. It is now between 115 and 120, on the weaker side.

Chart 4. EURUSD dollar cash prices. (Source: Data from Commodity Systems Inc)

The charts show that the dollar has been here before, but some investors want to protect against further losses. What is the best market?

Correlations

Correlations can tell you a lot. We calculate the daily returns of each cash price, then use the Excel function CORREL to create a cross-correlation table from 2017, shown in Chart 5. I’ve included COIN, the exchange that trades cryptocurrencies, and SPY, just in case!

Chart 5. Correlating gold and BTC-USD with the Euro currency and the Dollar Index. (Source: Data from Commodity System, Inc).

We first want to look at what markets are negatively correlated to the dollar index. We see the EURUSD, but prices are the reverse of DX, so that doesn’t count. But gold and the dollar index have a negative correlation. When gold goes up, the dollar index drops. That’s what we were looking for.

On the other hand, BTC-USD has the same relationship, but much weaker (-0.100 versus -0.346). In fact, we see that all of the markets are negatively correlated to DX. Note that, from a statistical viewpoint, small positive and negative correlations don’t mean much. The most important ones are gold-DX and gold-EURUSD. COIN vs SPY are positively correlated, no doubt because both have gone higher for different reasons.

Returns and Risk

Correlations are important, but we need to know about the returns and risk. Chart 6 shows both, plus the ratio of return to risk, which tells us which is the best payout. Bitcoin clearly has the best return, but at a risk that is most likely to wipe you out.

Chart 6. Annualized returns and annualized risk. (Source: Data from Commodity Systems Inc)

Gold turns out to be fairly stable, with the returns slightly better than the risk. Given that it is negatively correlated to the S&P (SPY), it is my choice for the best way to hedge the dollar. The other markets have very low returns; consequently, a lower ratio.

Combining a portfolio of SPY and physical gold seems ideal. But again, it is not without risk. Gold and the SPY can both decline at the same time, even if the long-term forecast looks promising. Given the choices, it is still the best.

data.title: I Bonds Look Attractive Right Now Amid Uncertainty: Buy Before November 1 Reset
data.description:
The BLS reported at 8:30 EST today that the 6-month annualized rate for September was 3.12% which combined with a fixed rate of 1.10% produces a total 6-month return of 4.22% for I Bonds purchased before the November 1 reset. Combined with the preceding 6-month total return of 3.98% the total return for the full 12 months will be 4.10%. A broad outline of implications follows.

In previous articles I have written about an unusual advantage an I Bond investor has in the days before the May 1 or November 1 reset. If you buy now, in advance of the November 1 reset, you will get the current fixed rate of 1.1% plus the variable (inflation adjusted) rate from November 1, 2024 through September 2025. Think about it. It's as if you were able to buy a stock or an index at its price a full year in the past with the one year return guaranteed. Who could say no to that advantage? If you don't like that return, you do nothing. If you like it, you pile in and buy as much as the allowable $10,000 and possibly more using the Gift Box.

Of course bond returns are minuscule compared to stock returns, but if you want to own any super-safe bonds, the current I Bond is a good option. It's particularly good as fixed rates are likely to change on November 1 in an unfavorable direction, most likely falling from 1.1% to 0.9%. Fixed income yields in general are more likely than not to fall as the Federal Reserve, under pressure from the President, cuts rates this year and probably continues well into next year. While it is always possible that rates at the longer end of the yield curve may stay as they are or even go up a bit it seems likely that rates on straight (non inflation adjusted) Treasury Notes up to 5 years will go down. There is consequently a likelihood that other sorts of fixed income such as CDs will follow.

If you want to play this, you should buy your desired quantity of I Bonds up to $10,000 as soon as possible, ideally early next week, so as not to get bumped beyond November 1. If you want to buy in a big way, you should take a last opportunity to make a pair with a trusted friend or family member and buy on a larger scale using the Gift Box. I have done this in the past and might do it again this year if I find an interested partner. But as with your own purchase you should do it well in advance of November 1, ideally by October 28, next Tuesday. That should give you enough time to think it over. Although you can still buy both your personal limit and the Gift Box amount later in the year after the reset, the fixed rate will likely be that 0.9% mentioned above. Students of I Bond fixed rates such as Dave Enna of Tipswatch have observed that since 2017 the I Bond fixed rate has been 65% of the real yield of the 5-year Treasury and the current calculation is so close to 0.95% that a minor shift may have nudged it either way, toward 0.9% or 1.0%. These sound like insignificant numbers but as the fixed rate on a current purchase will continue for the next 30 years that seemingly insignificant number adds up quite a bit. So buy now if you can or kick yourself later.

I should mention here that after the end of the year there will likely be major changes in the Gift Box and TreasuryDirect is "requesting" that Gift Box participants have their Gift Box I Bonds "delivered" by the end of the year. I myself and all Gift Box participants received a message from TreasuryDirect with this request, starting with the line "Treasury is always looking for ways to enhance your experience." That may well be true but the phone answerers at TreasuryDirect have a history of being somewhat disorganized in the face of major changes - this was especially so during the recent temporary but steep rise in inflation from 2021 through 2023, peaking at 9.1% in the middle of 2022 - and Treasury Direct was caught off guard both in their ability to fill purchases in a timely fashion and having not originally thought through the Gift Box and what impact major events might have on its use. As a result they must "suggest" actions that they can't really enforce. I am nevertheless planning to get together with my I Bond Gift Box partner and deliver our Gift I Bonds to each other.

Details Alluded To In The Above Paragraphs With Details on How I Bonds Work to Follow

The present government shutdown produced a one-week delay, the Bureau of Labor Statistics assured that the data used was accurate and based on the same statistical measures as usual. If you buy an I Bond before November 1 (remember: try to be a few days early), you will receive the variable rate for November 1, 2024, through April 30, 2025, plus the variable rate from May 1, 2025, through September 30, 2025. To state the obvious, the last month included is not the current month (October) but the last month for which full data is available (September). A favorable quirk in the TreasuryDirect I Bond rules is that I Bonds purchase at any time of the month will receive interest for the full month. Purchasing now therefore gets a little extra in return.

As mentioned earlier the fixed rate for both six month periods is 1.1% and a major reason to buy now is that the fixed rate starting November 1 is certain to decline. The variable rate from last November 1 through April 30, 2025, was 2.86% so that adding the fixed rate of 1.10% the overall return for the first six-month period was 3.98%. With today's CPI report we now know that the variable rate for May 1 through September 30 will be 3.12% and adding in the 1.10% fixed rate produces a 4.22% number for the just ended six months.

Averaging the two numbers produces a number of 4.10% return for the entire year before adding in the relatively small compounding. In any case that's an excellent number for a flexible asset which you can get out of in a year and three months by sacrificing one quarter (three months) of returns. If interested in short term return but flexible, you might wait to choose a three-month period with low inflation and low variable returns. It substantially beats the yield of any other super-safe asset for a year and a quarter. It gets better from there as one can depart after five years with no sacrifice. You can then consider year to year for the next 30 years whether it remains a good deal. For the record I have never cashed in an I Bond and don't intend to in the future.

At the present moment most analysts who bother to think about it seem to feel that inflation will likely remain about the same as the present to the distant event horizon. Today's Big Money poll in Barron's did exactly that, with the majority expecting 3% as far as the eye can see. Your response to that will depend upon your point of view. I'll be long gone in 30 years (being 81) but my daughter who is the designated heir for my I Bonds will likely be in her early 80s. I have explained to her how I Bonds worked and given her the cash to buy an I Bond on her own, a task that she performed successfully. A lot can happen in 30 years, especially when it comes to inflation. If your need is "inflation insurance," you would do well to consider the amount of U.S. government debt which is likely to be engaged by some form of money printing which will bring with it major inflation. I don't actually see my I Bond portfolio as insurance that you can cash and take to the grocery store, but more as an asset class you will be happy to have as part of your total portfolio if major inflation takes place. I have purchased I Bonds regularly since the year 2000 and they have performed spectacularly to that goal.

The present moment has more than the normal degree of uncertainty. President Trump's policies could produce anything from inflation to deflation, maybe one after the other, and I Bonds have a wonderful quality talked about in the final section below. They are structured to protect you against both inflation and deflation.

For what it's worth the 100-plus years of good records show that inflation has a very long term average of 3% although that 3% average puts together a lumpy and bumpy history running from the extreme deflation of the 1930s to the extreme inflation of the 1970s which I myself experienced. I will continue to buy I Bonds as long as they are offered. The ones I was fortunate to buy starting in 2000 have amounted to a sum I won't boast about. I actually bought them casually, almost on a whim at a time when the stock market looked awful and overpriced and soon crashed spectacularly. So buy I Bonds by early next week if they meet your personal goals.

In writing about I Bonds I assume that the majority of my readers are already well informed about the way they work. On the other hand, a few will be new to I Bonds so I always include some form of the following section giving a sort of beginner's version on how I Bonds work and how they resemble and differ from similar assets. Even if you have known about I Bonds for a long time you may want to refresh your memory in case you have forgotten some details. I do so myself whenever writing a new article on I Bonds.

What Are I Bonds And How Do They Work?

I Bonds are a form of US Treasury Savings Bond which is inflation-adjusted. Like ordinary EE Savings Bonds, I Bonds are backed by the United States Treasury. Savings bonds have been around since 1935. Under a temporary name change to War Bonds, US Savings Bonds helped fund World War II. I Bonds have been around since 1998.

I Bonds produce returns with two components. The first is a fixed rate which provides the "real" return - real meaning inflation adjusted. For new purchases, this rate resets semiannually on May 1 and Nov. 1, or the next business day if the first day of the month happens to fall on a holiday. Once you purchase an I Bond, the real rate in force at that time continues for 30 years until the bond matures or until the owner chooses to redeem it. The second component of I Bond return is the inflation rate as represented by the Urban Consumer Price Index (CPI-U), the same index that is used for Social Security resets. The inflation rate used for I Bonds is reset semiannually at the same time the fixed rate is reset, on May 1 and Nov. 1, and the accrued value of your I Bond is updated so that the return compounds semiannually. Note that the fixed rate and the variable rate are reset every six months, the variable rate from the CPI report, the fixed rate at the whim of the Treasury which has sometimes been thought to track the five or ten-year Treasury note.

You can currently buy up to $10,000 annually of electronic I Bonds in a TreasuryDirect account - that's $10,000 per Social Security Number so that you can buy the same amount for your spouse and each of your children. In the past, also you could also request that up to $5000 of your Federal income tax refund be received in the form of a paper I Bond which will be mailed to you whenever the IRS gets around to sending it. That possibility was phased out, however, on January 1, 2025.

Like all Treasury Bonds, I Bonds are not taxable by states and municipalities. Another advantage of I Bonds is that you can choose to defer paying Federal taxes until maturity or redemption. This can be helpful if you or your heir (whom you designate with each purchase) expect to have lower taxable income at some point in the future. Under some circumstances, I Bonds are tax-free when used for educational purposes - read the fine print, though.

One of the most important but seldom noticed qualities of I Bonds is that they're not only an excellent inflation hedge but a hedge against deflation as well. The reason is that the inflation component of the composite return never dips below zero. In the event of a deflationary period, the accrued inflation value of the I Bond would simply freeze at its highest level. When inflation returns, as it always has, the total value of the account begins to compound again from the highest level it has reached.

I Bonds are probably the most flexible investment available. This is because their duration is any date you choose, from one year to their maturity date of 30 years. You cannot redeem your I Bond during the first year, but after that, you choose any moment you wish if for any reason you want to get out. You can thus use them as a place to park cash for the short to intermediate term, or leave them in place and continue to have an assured long-term hedge against inflation.

For the first five years, the price paid for redeeming a bond is giving up one quarter (three months) of returns. After five years of owning an I Bond, the forfeiting of one quarter's returns no longer applies. There are, by the way, no commissions or other costs involved in buying or selling I Bonds. Paper bonds - now available only as part of your tax refund - can be bought at price intervals from $50 to $10,000 while electronic bonds may be bought in penny increments starting at $25. You could, for example, buy an I Bond with a face value $25.23. The Treasury Direct site has a slightly more detailed description which I also recommend that you read. If you have questions I will do my best to respond in comments.
